ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork-and-garlic-chive filling; mapped to broader pork dish class FOODON_03302757.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for pork-and-garlic-chive dumpling filling; A0CKP (pork meat products) used as closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork and garlic chive filling is approximately 100 g, equivalent to the filling content of 4–5 medium-sized dumplings (jiaozi) or 2 medium steamed buns (baozi).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground Pork, Raw (FDC ID 168351) and Garlic Chives, Raw (FDC ID 169230); values proportionally combined and adjusted for seasoning additions
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for pork-and-garlic-chive filling as a composite dish. Values are derived by proportional combination of USDA ground pork (raw, ~75% lean) and garlic chives (raw), with sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce and seasoning additions typical of this preparation. The input AI-generated values (energy 220 kcal, protein 12.8 g, fat 17.2 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g) are consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and have been retained. Saturated fat estimated from USDA ground pork fatty acid profile (~6.4 g per 100 g of filling). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite filling is not available in USDA FoodData Central. Cholesterol estimated at 55 mg per 100 g based on USDA ground pork data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat-and-Vegetable Preparations — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate pork-dominant filling; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork-and-garlic-chive filling in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on the very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and high protein-and-fat composition of the filling. The estimate is consistent with GI values reported for low-carbohydrate meat-dominant preparations in Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (25 × 3.2) / 100 = 0.8, classified as very low. When consumed with wheat dumpling wrappers, the overall meal GI increases substantially.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (410 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce and seasoning, combined with moderate saturated fat from ground pork
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion sizes of this filling and request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparations; the 410 mg sodium per 100 g filling (before wrapper contribution) can represent a significant fraction of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (~25), with high protein supporting postprandial satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: The filling itself is low in carbohydrates and has a low glycemic index, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions; however, total meal glycemic load increases significantly when the filling is enclosed in wheat-flour dumpling wrappers, which should be accounted for in carbohydrate counting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~6.4 g per 100 g) from ground pork and elevated sodium (410 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this filling due to its saturated fat and sodium content; choosing leaner pork mince, reducing soy sauce, and limiting serving size to 80–100 g of filling per meal are practical strategies to reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and sodium content may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this dish in moderation; while garlic chives and ginger provide some anti-inflammatory phytonutrients, the overall high saturated fat and sodium profile of the pork-dominant filling may exacerbate systemic inflammation if consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork and Garlic Chives (猪肉韭菜, Zhū Ròu Jiǔ Cài) is a classic Northern Chinese filling used in jiaozi (boiled or pan-fried dumplings) and baozi (steamed buns). The filling combines coarsely ground or minced pork — typically 70–80% lean — with finely chopped garlic chives (Allium tuberosum), a flat-leafed allium with a distinctly pungent, garlicky, and slightly peppery aroma that is far more intense than common chives. The mixture is seasoned with fresh ginger, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and optionally a small amount of Sichuan pepper, producing a bold, savory, and aromatic filling. The texture is moist and slightly coarse, with the garlic chives providing fibrous contrast to the tender pork. Nutritionally, the filling is energy-dense at approximately 220 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (~17 g) and moderate protein (~12.8 g), while carbohydrates are very low (~3.2 g), yielding an estimated glycemic index of approximately 25. Sodium is elevated at 410 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce, making this a high-sodium preparation. The filling is a good source of vitamin K (from garlic chives), vitamin A, thiamine, niacin, and selenium. Clinically, the filling is low-glycemic and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when portion-controlled, but the high sodium and saturated fat content require caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is a staple at Chinese New Year celebrations, family gatherings, and everyday meals across Northern China, and is widely consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for pork-and-garlic-chive filling as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central ground pork and garlic chive entries, proportionally combined and adjusted for seasoning. The AI-generated input values are consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein-and-fat composition of the filling, referenced against Atkinson et al. (2008) in Diabetes Care. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite pork-and-garlic-chive filling are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source as of April 2026. 2. Saturated fat (6.4 g) and cholesterol (55 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground pork (75% lean) fatty acid profile and have been proportionally adjusted for the filling composition. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific filling exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = 0.8 (very low). 4. The input source cited (Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, http://www.caas.cn/) is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2 and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central is used instead. 5. The portions array in the input contained a duplicate entry; deduplicated to a single 100 g serving. 6. This record covers the filling only, not the complete dumpling or bun with wrapper; total meal nutrition will differ substantially when wheat wrappers are included. 7. Halal status is marked false as the preparation may include non-halal pork; consumers should verify sourcing.
